narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, an LSAT score of 31, upper-middle family income, and plans to attend full-time, along with your school decile rankings from Year 1 and Year 3 both sitting at the lowest level of 1—well below the typical student's averages around the mid-5s. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated a 91.7% chance that you would fail the bar exam, leading directly to the rejection of your application. This high failure probability means the school views your profile as carrying substantial risk compared to applicants predicted to pass, even though your full-time status aligns with the vast majority of students. The decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 school decile ranking, as being in the bottom decile strongly signaled to the model a higher likelihood of bar failure, pushing the prediction firmly in that direction. Your Year 1 decile ranking at the bottom level reinforced this concern, adding further weight toward the failure outcome, unlike the stronger early performances seen in most admitted students. Even your upper-middle family income, while a positive factor on average, wasn't enough to offset these academic signals and had a smaller pull toward passing. Overall, your grades placed you notably below typical students—whose undergraduate GPAs average 3.21 and LSATs 36.7—making your profile stand out as riskier in the model's eyes. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven assessment focused on bar passage trends. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the average, strengthening your high school decile performance through additional coursework or programs, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience as a Hispanic student pursuing law. Exploring community college or targeted prep courses could help rebuild your academic deciles before reapplying. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these key areas head-on. With focused effort, you can shift your profile toward the passing predictions that open doors. Keep pushing—your determination will make the difference.
